data.title: Longleaf Partners Small-Cap Fund Q1 2026 Portfolio Review
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Longleaf Partners Small-Cap Fund Q1 2026 Commentary.

Notable Contributors & Detractors

Boston Beer (SAM) – Alcoholic beverage company Boston Beer was a contributor for the quarter as industry data improved compared to last year. Sentiment around the industry appears to be leveling off after two years of headwinds from changes in consumption habits. The company reported solid first quarter results that were highlighted by continued improvement in gross margins. Recent innovation Sun Cruiser is growing volumes rapidly as the brand expands nationwide to take advantage of the sizable runway remaining in the ready-to-drink tea space. The company also continues to make progress in fixing problems at Twisted Tea, their largest brand. Boston Beer remains both an active share repurchaser and attractive acquisition target as alcohol industry consolidation looks to be on the horizon.

Shenandoah Telecommunications (SHEN) – Telecom company Shenandoah was a contributor for the quarter. The company reported a solid set of results that helped the market wake up to these off-the-radar assets that we started buying last year. While parts of this unique company are technically “legacy” telecom, Shenandoah made the wise call to focus on building out its fiber business years ago. This led to decreased short-term earnings power and a higher leverage ratio on reported EBITDA, but the company built a substantial amount of value with this capital allocation decision. Now a strong majority of its value comes from this great, growing business. Shenandoah also wisely securitized most of its fiber assets in the 4th quarter to improve the balance sheet. The company is now getting closer to FCF positive in 2027, and earnings growth should continue to be strong thereafter.

Empire State Realty (ESRT) and Alexander’s (ALX) – We wrote about these two New York real estate companies together in a previous note and will now do so again. Their stock prices moved in different directions this quarter. To the positive, Alexander’s announced a great sale of a non-core, non-income-generating asset in Queens that puts the company further on offense. While ESRT is also actively looking to sell assets, they did not have any news on that front in the quarter and therefore declined with other real estate companies as fears about AI-driven job losses hit the sector. International travel trends also continued to impact their Observatory business. In spite of these headwinds, ESRT reported solid leasing results and should be able to grow cash flow this year. Both companies still have irreplaceable assets driven by aligned partners. Their divergent stock price moves led to us trimming Alexander’s and adding to ESRT as the quarter went on.

Rayonier (RYN) and PotlatchDeltic (PCH) – Timberland companies Rayonier and PotlatchDeltic completed their merger in the quarter to become our largest single position in the Fund. While the combination of the pre-merger-close price move up in PotlatchDeltic and the post-merger-close price move down in Rayonier did not combine to make this a large detractor, it is worth noting the post-merger weakness we believe did not line up with the solid value creation opportunity at a company like this. While some of the sell-off was likely short-term/technical (index rebalancing), timberland remains a great and long-term store of value. Interest rate moves and housing market sentiment can impact perception in the short term, but this asset class has historically been an inflation hedge that has performed well in a variety of environments. Post-merger, Rayonier has a strong balance sheet and the ability to go on offense. We believe that targeted asset sales and share repurchase can drive value per share growth from here. We also wrote about the company in our most recent Research Perspectives note .

Becle (BCCLF) – Spirits company Becle detracted after the company reported a quarter that the stock market did not like. This short-term earnings volatility (on a business that is 200+ years old) was driven by a shift in distribution that is the right thing to do for the long-term. While this means that reported revenues will be down a few percent in 2026, this sets the company up for growth in 2027 and beyond. As mentioned above on Boston Beer, industry trends did not get worse, but there remains debate about spirits consumption going forward. The biggest industry news came late in the quarter when it was confirmed that Pernod Ricard and Brown-Forman were discussing a merger. The industry is ripe for consolidation, and now this could be kicking off. Becle will be poised to act from a position of financial strength while being focused on the still fastest growing part of the spirits industry, tequila. We added to our position in the quarter after the stock declined.

Mattel (MAT) – Children's toy, media, and consumer products creator Mattel was a detractor in the quarter. The stock fell due to an unexpected $150 million (15% of EBITDA) in incremental spending on initiatives including mobile gaming, Brick Shop (Mattel's competitor to LEGO), and direct to consumer marketing. CEO Ynon Kreiz cited a one-year payback on this spend, but the market remains in show-me mode and the price declined proportionate to the 2026 earnings per share guidance reduction. This was compounded by 4Q results that missed expectations, especially in the US. To the positive, the company committed to $1.5 billion in share repurchase over the next 3 years, which equates to 33% of shares outstanding at today's price. 2026 should have demonstrated the true FCF power of Mattel given traction on IP initiatives including two new movies (Masters of the Universe and Matchbox), two new mobile game launches, and licensing momentum with Toy Story 5 and KPop Demon Hunters. Unfortunately, this was delayed, and we are focused on what we can do to improve this situation.

Tripadvisor (TRIP) – Digital experiences, dining and travel company Tripadvisor was a detractor for the quarter. As previously mentioned, we were pleased to see board improvement at the company. Unfortunately, this came after a disappointing quarter and a lack of board urgency up to this point. The legacy Tripadvisor business has been worse than we first thought a year ago. However, it has gone from only ~20% of our value then to now closer to 10%, while Viator and TheFork have grown. Macro travel trends got worse in the quarter as well. To the positive, we believe that the company is now acting on what is important to realize value per share. There are a variety of strategic options available to this company, and we look forward to how this year develops.

Portfolio Activity

During the quarter we had no new purchases or exits. We remain disciplined in our search for businesses that meet our Business, People, and Price criteria and are close to buying multiple new investments that you could hear about next quarter.

Before investing in any Longleaf Partners Fund, you should carefully consider the Fund's investment objectives, risks, charges, and expenses. For a current Prospectus and Summary Prospectus, which contain this and other important information, visit https://regdocs.blugiant.com/longleaf/# . Please read the Prospectus and Summary Prospectus carefully before investing.

Risks

The Longleaf Small-Cap Fund is subject to stock market risk, meaning stocks in the Fund may fluctuate in response to developments at individual companies or due to general market and economic conditions. Also, because the Fund generally invests in 15 to 25 companies, share value could fluctuate more than if a greater number of securities were held. Smaller company stocks may be more volatile with less financial resources than those of larger companies.

The Russell 2000 Index measures the performance of the 2000 smallest companies in the Russell 3000 Index. The Russell 3000 represents approximately 97% of the American public equity market and the Russell 2000 represents 10% of the Russell 3000. An index is unmanaged, does not reflect the deduction of fees or expenses, and cannot be invested in directly.

P/V ("price to value") is a calculation that compares the prices of the stocks in a portfolio to Southeastern's appraisal of their intrinsic values. The ratio represents a single data point about a Fund and should not be construed as something more. P/V does not guarantee future results, and we caution investors not to give this calculation undue weight.

"Margin of Safety" is a reference to the difference between a stock's market price and Southeastern's calculated appraisal value. It is not a guarantee of investment performance or returns.

Free Cash Flow (FCF) is a measure of a company's ability to generate the cash flow necessary to maintain operations. Generally, it is calculated as operating cash flow minus capital expenditures.

Price / Earnings (P/E) is the ratio of a company's share price compared to its earnings per share.

Enterprise value (EV) is a company's market capitalization plus debt, minority interest and preferred shares, and less total cash and cash equivalents.

PE multiple is a financial metric that frames a company's current stock price in terms of the company's earnings per share.

REIT is a Real Estate Investment Trust which is a company that owns, operates, or finances income-producing real estate.

A Basis Point is one hundredth of one percent.

Mergers and Acquisitions (M&A) are business transactions in which the ownership of a company, business organization, or one of their operating units is transferred to or consolidated with another entity.

As of March 31, 2026, the top ten holdings for the Longleaf Partners Small-Cap Fund: Rayonier, Inc. 8.1%; Becle S.A.B. de C.V. 7.6%; GCI Liberty, Inc. 7.5%; Tripadvisor, Inc. 7.0%; Mattel, Inc. 6.8%; Shenandoah Telecommunications Company 6.5%; CNX Resources Corporation 5.6%; Empire State Realty Trust, Inc. 4.8%; White Mountains Insurance Group Ltd. 4.8% and Graham Holdings Company 4.8%. Fund holdings are subject to change and holdings discussions are not recommendations to buy or sell any security. Current and future holdings are subject to risk.

Funds distributed by ALPS Distributors, Inc.

LLP001631 expires 7/15/2026

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: This V-Bottom Rally Could Be Quickly Forming Another Peak
data.description:
Market Turned From Extreme Bearish To Extreme Bullish?

The market is now at an all-time high, even though the war hasn’t yet quite reached a definitive conclusion. Does it surprise you?

If anything, I think it goes to prove that that's just how the forward looking market really works.

Sure, we have not reached a permanent ceasefire. But it's not like it really matters to the market right now.

Especially not when you see the market reverse into new highs from a bottom just about 2 weeks ago.

The market seems to be telling us that the whatever happens in the Middle East no longer matters that much once we are firmly past the worst stages of it.

Has it also stunned the media? You bet. But, it's not like the market really cares, right?

Markets Don't Need 100% Clarity To Turn Bullish

I thought this commentary by Goldman Sachs (GS) aptly summarized what the market is thinking versus what the media is worried about.

The media wants a clear conclusion, but experienced investors should be able to tell us that the market had already discounted them in late March.

It’s arguably true that there are still a laundry list of uncertainties that could really find their way back into the narrative later.

But, it’s also crucial for us to recognize that the market doesn’t quite need absolute clarity to regain control of the bullish skew, right?

Think about it. In the absolute mess of the COVID supply chain snafu, the market continued to rally until it became a tech and growth bubble. But, that only happened like when? As a reminder, that was late 2021.

And then last year’s tariffs. It was only earlier this year that President Trump’s tariffs were struck down by SCOTUS. Although, there could be ways around it to get them reinstated by July, as Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent opined recently.

Yet, the market rallied for an incredible six month period for growth and tech through late 2025, before the reallocation themes then occurred as investors switched focus from growth to value.

But, that value narrative then took a steep tumble (sans energy stocks), as the Middle Eastern conflict unfolded.

However, even as the ceasefire negotiations continue hanging over the horizon, as parties negotiate to extend them, the market is already looking past it.

Did investors get it right?

But Being Extremely Bullish Too Quickly Is Also A Problem

A V-bottom is a very powerful event.

There’s no doubt that CTAs went into the April bottom at nearly max bearish, as seen above.

The metric appears to have flipped completely to the upside as I pen this write-up, which corroborates the V-bottom market print that we observed.

With such an extreme positioning into the April lows, the sudden narrative shift likely also caused them to go long rapidly, spurring the momentum spike that we are seeing now.

Market strategists have also gone bullish, generally speaking.

And during this time, Wall Street hasn't even downgraded the market's earnings growth outlook.

I guess it shows how confident the analysts have been on the underlying strength of the companies in the market.

And it seems like nothing could quite unhinge the AI infrastructure buildout themes that have driven this broad-based recovery.

But, given how quickly the recovery has panned out, it's also sensible for us to assess whether such a positioning shift has also been taken out of context already?

Also, on the CTAs. As the flows have shifted into extreme positioning, we must now consider whether a slowdown is due, or potentially even reverse?

Moreover, as these extreme bearish positions get unwound so quickly, it has also translated into the market print, right?

These effects are also shown clearly in the collapse of the liquidity premium in just the past two weeks. If anything, we now have clear confirmation that the market has moved resoundingly back into the risk-on mode.

V-Bottom Prints Are Usually Strong Bottoms

Normally, I’m in favor of V-bottoms. We have had a few notable prints in the past ten years.

The COVID one in March/April of 2020 was a clear example. And last year’s Liberation Day print was another one to remember.

I think the market has been proven right again with how the 2026 bottom formed.

Nonetheless, there are many things to worry about; I'm not saying things are crystal clear now.

These include how the supply chain will rejiggle itself, as we don’t quite have clear answers into the shipping flows through the Strait of Hormuz with the naval blockade.

Former IMF Chief Economist & Harvard University Professor Kenneth Rogoff made the case for us to consider that we may not quite be prepared for worse shocks that could be making their way into the economy.

Next, we also don’t quite know whether the unemployment related to AI will ultimately have a more pernicious effect on consumer spending.

However, based on recent studies, that doesn’t seem to be the case yet.

Although these are really rapidly evolving dynamics, and I think it is worth closely watching.

While the results from Wall Street’s leading banks show that consumer spending stays robust, these changes might not have been captured into the future outlook if the unemployment rate continues to worsen.

And we already see how companies like Block (XYZ) and now Snap (SNAP) are using AI as their primary motive to slash headcounts.

Companies could be emboldened to use the AI narrative to get in shape, and we don't quite yet know how pervasive these things could get. But, it's definitely worth watching.

But A Topping Signal Could Also Be Forthcoming

And then, price action.

My bullish undertones from the start of April have now quickly shifted to a more neutral leaning, as the market came back much quicker than I had anticipated.

The print is also starting to flash caution for me, because it might indicate that the extreme bearish positioning has given way to extreme bullish positioning.

I’m not saying that this presages the next bear market, but it also indicates that the market has quickly thrown the tail risk scenarios out of the window too quickly. Does that make sense?

To some extent, yes. The conflict is likely past the worst stages, I believe.

However, to say that there wouldn’t be any effects and think that the market shouldn’t discount supply chain risks, especially relating to the shipping flows through the Strait, doesn't sound reasonable to me.

A slower and steadier recovery would have been the much more preferred advancing move.

That allows the market more time to digest the developments.

This stark shift from very bearish to very bullish has arguably taken the narratives way too far all of a sudden.

At this point, the market looks like it wants to get that decisive breakout, but I’m not convinced.

Until I see it print, if you managed to get in very aggressively in the first two weeks of April, this will be the moment where I will urge you to hold back your horses.

There will be a time to get back aggressively again, but not unless the market shows that it isn’t a double top bearish reversal print in the making.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: ProPetro: A Re-Rating Gone Too Far, Too Fast
data.description:
Elevator Thesis

Oil remains unstable as Iran and U.S. tensions continue to push the prices up and down with each headline. Under normal conditions, this arrangement would provide oilfield services with some support.

However, this cycle is different as U.S. shale producers are not chasing growth anymore. They concentrate on the capital discipline and free cash flow rather than volume. The change has disrupted the connection between oil prices and service demand.

This brings me to ProPetro Holding (PUMP) which is a pretty intriguing case.

ProPetro operates in the Permian Basin and earns most of its revenue from hydraulic fracturing. In simple words, it helps other companies extract oil and gas from underground.

Additionally, its management is attempting to develop a second growth leg by PROPWR. This segment basically targets power generation and infrastructure contracts of longer duration.

Moreover, its Q4 results were pretty mixed.

Revenue clocked in at $290 million, a decrease of about 9-10% annually. Meanwhile, adjusted EBITDA was close to $51 million, with margin of around 18%.

Therefore, the activity is still soft. Furthermore, the earnings beat appeared solid but it was rather due to efficiency gains than to actual demand recovery.

Interestingly, PUMP shares have skyrocketed over 175% YOY (as of writing), but 2026 expectations of EPS remain a little negative at approximately -$0.13.

The market is obviously beginning to price in a shift towards a more infrastructure-linked business model.

In general, that is the core tension for me. The business is progressing, yet earnings are yet to follow. This gap ends up justifying my Hold rating at the current levels.

Core Business: Cash Flow Strong, Growth Absent

ProPetro is using its traditional fracturing business as a cash cow. It is being used to squeeze out as much money as possible to pay for a future in power.

The above snapshot is interesting. We can clearly see that the company’s revenue has consistently decreased throughout the year.

Let’s put this into perspective. In Q1 25, its revenue came in at $359 million which decreased around 20% to $290 million in Q4.

This means that the U.S. shale producers have diverted their focus from just drilling aggressively. They are now more interested in creating FCF and returning capital to shareholders.

This change effectively caps the demand for ProPetro’s traditional services. The "drill at any cost" era is over and the company’s ability to hike prices is over with it, too.

Given the declining revenue, the 18% EBITDA margin is a huge win. It shows that ProPetro has perfected the art of cost discipline. The management is pushing into its Permian dominance to extract even the last penny out of already existing fleets.

The actual disconnection is that it generated $232 million in operating cash flow for FY 25 but only $1 million in net income. The money is real, whereas the accounting profits are low due to extensive depreciation and high expense of maintaining old equipment. It is a company that generates cash but cannot present the profits on paper.

What is more important is that it is no longer an independent growth narrative. The management is shifting capital to PROPWR.

The FY 26 total capex guidance is a massive $390-$435 million. Out of this, a whopping $250-$275 million is assigned to the power segment while trimming traditional completions spending to $140-$160 million.

That said, internal cash flow alone is not enough to bridge the needs. ProPetro has also raised about $163 million by issuing 15 million shares at a price of roughly $10. This has added dilution while helping in expansion needs.

I think this validates the fact that the core business is stable and cash-generative, however, its mission has changed to fund the transformation.

PROPWR: The Growth Driver Behind the Narrative

The market is now viewing PROPWR like the "golden goose" that justifies every bit of the 160% YOY rally.

PUMP’s power segment already has 240 MW of committed capacity, and plans to expand to more than 1 GW by 2030.

This is a straight play on the huge electrification of the Permian and the increasing data center demand. It is a brilliant twist because it converts a cyclical service company into a quasi-utility.

The economics of this are higher compared to the frac world. We are considering long-term, recurring contracts which do not change with each $5 change in oil. Consequently, potential EBITDA per MW is much greater in comparison to traditional margins.

This is what investors are seeking in terms of better quality earnings. The demand is structural and permanent, as opposed to a momentary stage in the cycle.

However, let’s be real about the execution. The market is already pricing in huge success, but project remains in its initial phases. Alongside, the management is committing to a capital spending of $390-$435 million to get there. Before the cash starts coming back, that is a lot of money to spend.

The other significant layer is the way in which this growth is being funded. ProPetro is also using external equipment financing, such as working with Caterpillar Financial Services.

It brings PROPWR closer to an asset-backed infrastructure model. In this kind of structure, growth occurs at the equipment level instead of being completely backed by company’s balance sheet.

Though it should be noted that this does not eliminate core execution risk. The real outcome is still determined by the demand, utilization, and contract economics.

Valuation Discussion

PUMP stock ballooned a mind boggling ∼178% over the last twelve months. Nevertheless, it did not go up without a hitch.

The upswing was largely driven by a sharp breakout in late 2025, which pushed the stock into the 13-14 range.

The simple money has been made as the market has already digested the power pivot theme.

The stock is trading very close to its 52-week high of $15. It resembles a very optimistic set-up with less upside available.

Let’s see where the mismatch exists. The 2026 consensus earnings per share are estimated at negative 0.13. The company is still likely to record accounting losses despite the stock trading at multi-year highs.

Apparently, this weakness is already being looked through by the market to 2027. This still leaves a prominent gap in expectations that is quite risky.

In effect, investors are buying back their recovery, two years down the road. Any failure in execution, be it due to supply chain problems or slower electrification, could soon strain the valuation.

Moreover, the stock is not cheap at a forward P/S of 1.36x. Indeed, this is due to the uncertainty surrounding the transition and timing of earnings recovery.

Now, let’s assess some scenarios.

The bear case will result in the stock going down to the $8-$10 range. This occurs when the legacy frac business decelerates at a greater pace than PROPWR can scale. It is also more probable when elevated expenditure consumes cash before PROPWR begins to produce decent returns. Then, further dilution would be required, which would damage the valuation at hand.

The base case maintains the stock at $12-$15. In this case, the legacy business remains strong to finance the transition. At the same time, the market waits for 2027 earnings to come through. This remains a ‘show me’ story. The valuation is kept at a limit until backlog is converted into real earnings.

In contrast, the bull case points to $18 or higher. This would require a non-oilfield win of a massive magnitude. It would also refocus valuation to be more of a utility-based multiple versus a service multiple of close to 4x EBITDA.

The risk-reward appears to be stretched overall. Mistakes can be very costly at present levels. Investors are investing in future performance instead of the recent results.

Key Points to Watch and Takeaway

The upcoming Q1 26 earnings report will be a crucial checkpoint.

In case legacy frac revenue is notably less than anticipated, it would begin to undermine the notion that this segment can reliably finance the transition.

Meanwhile, it will be interesting to see how much cash is already pumped into PROPWR.

Then, the emphasis would naturally draw towards execution which remains a central risk.

The current valuation already values successful scaling. Hence, reduced momentum in deployments or slower contract ramp would soon become a load on sentiment.

That is directly related to capital intensity. In case the project costs drift out of control or the project needs some extra funding, transition may be more dilution-intensive. It might also push a meaningful contribution to earnings after 2027.

This risk is even more apparent when we see that 550 MW of equipment has already been ordered.

Lastly, sentiment is also closely related to the AI and power demand theme. In case the expectations regarding data center or Permian electrification falter, the stock would lose its premium. It may then revert to a more conservative oilfield services valuation.

It is easy to be carried away by the excitement when a stock rises over 2.75x within a year.

ProPetro is an incredible story, and the shift to power infrastructure is what the Permian wants. But you need not tie the quality of the idea and the price of the stock together. At $15 per share, you are paying an enormous premium on a transformation that is yet to be tested.

The company is cashflow positive but is technically weak in terms of profitability, which becomes a very thin tightrope.

All in all, for those who are already invested, keep your positions. Concurrently, those who want a piece of PUMP for their portfolios should wait for a better entry point.

data.title: IEMG: Surprising Resilience In The Face Of Macro Headwinds
data.description:
This analysis serves as a follow up to an article that I wrote last year for the iShares Core MSCI Emerging Markets ETF (IEMG). My rating for the fund was a Sell, not because I was recommending shorting the ETF but rather just avoiding it in favor of other international options. In hindsight, my analysis was a miss. Since that article was published on May 12, IEMG has gained just over 39% when dividend are included. I had stated a preference for more developed markets at that time, suggesting alternatives like the Vanguard FTSE Developed Markets Index Fund ETF (VEA) and the Schwab International Equity ETF (SCHF). Those recommendations did not look like inferior options until the last four months or so when IEMG really began to outperform.

International stocks from all parts of the world continue to outperform the counterparts from the US. What was a trendy pick for 2025 has continued to show strength in 2026. However, there are reasons for concern that go beyond the obvious issues that have been created by the current military conflict in the Middle East. Global inflation rates are trending higher. Commodity prices are rising, and fiscal policy, especially in emerging economies, is constrained by the need to limit expansion of public debt.

In this analysis, I will revisit my last look at IEMG and consider what the remainder of this year may hold for emerging markets. My previous assessment of a Sell for the fund may have been too harsh, even though emerging markets continue to carry a great deal of risk. I now consider the ETF to be a Hold based on the data presented below.

ETF Overview

According to the fund’s prospectus, the iShares Core MSCI Emerging Markets ETF is designed to follow the investment results of an index composed of large-, mid-, and small-capitalization stocks from emerging markets. IEMG is passively managed, tracking the contents of the MSCI Emerging Markets Investable Market Index. This index is comprised of 3079 stocks from 24 emerging market countries. Its manager, BlackRock, states that the purpose of the fund is to be used as a core allocation for a portfolio “to diversify internationally and seek long-term growth”.

When I last wrote about the iShares Core MSCI Emerging Markets ETF, it held $85.6 billion in assets under management. Today, less than 12 months later, the fund’s AUM is 71.8% higher at $147.1 billion. This growth, of course, is due in part to the performance of the fund itself as well as the attraction is has earned in the market after such impressive recent returns.

As it did last May, IEMG still owns about 400 stocks fewer than its benchmark index. While the fund may be diversified from the perspective of its total number of holdings, it is heavily weighted toward the top of its 10 largest allocations. Eleven months ago, Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM) had a weight of 7.9%. As of the close of the market on April 14, its share of the fund’s assets had grown to 12.1%. TSM’s share price has risen over 143% in the last 12 months, benefitting from the boom in AI and data center spending. Samsung Electronics and SK Hynix are second and third on the ETF’s top 10 holdings with a weight of 5.0% and 3.1%, respectively. Both are Korean companies that currently do not have ADR listings in the US, although SK Hynix is considering a US listing that could take place in the second half of this year. Beyond Tencent Holdings (TCEHY) and Alibaba Group (BABA), no other stock in the fund has an allocation greater than 1%. This means the fund’s top five holdings make up just over 25% of the fund’s assets, leaving the remaining 75% to the remaining 2,652.

As might be expected from a glance at its top 10 holdings, IEMG is heavily weighted toward the tech sector. The top three holdings are all technology companies and comprise 20.25% of the fund’s assets on their own. The entire tech sector has an allocation of just under a third of the fund overall. The financial sector, which had two holdings in the fund’s top 10, is the only other sector with a double-digit allocation at 19.5%. Consumer discretionary, which includes BABA, is third in sector allocation at 9.7%.

IEMG has a dividend yield of 2.42%. Distributions are made twice each year, once in June and once in December with the latter being the larger of the two. The fund has been able to produce dividend growth of at least 10.5% for the last three years.

Analysis of IEMG

As mentioned in my introduction, the last year has been a very good one for shareholders of IEMG. The 32.6% returns posted by the fund in 2025 were its best since 2017. However, the fund’s volatility can be seen in the chart below that shows its annual gains and losses dating back to that year. Investors who joined the fund on January 1, 2021, had a loss of just over 5% of their investment by the end of 2024 when dividends were not considered. The last time the fund had a year like the last one with gains of at least 30%, IEMG lost almost 15% of its net asset value the following year in 2018.

When its performance is compared to other funds in its category, the iShares Core MSCI Emerging Markets ETF appears to hover just around the 50-percentile range. Over the last decade, the fund has never finished in the top quartile for its category, but it has also never been in the bottom quartile. Even this year, when the fund has posted strong gains in the first three and a half months of 2026, the NAV gain for IEMG of 12.73% places it only in the 58th percentile.

If there is one clear positive aspect about IEMG, it is its expense ratio of 0.09%. This would be competitive when compared to domestic funds, but for an international fund that often buys and sells stocks in undeveloped markets, this is surprisingly low. The fund benefits from a low turnover rate of 6.0%. The index that it follows is rebalanced only twice a year, reducing some needs for more frequent transactions. Stocks included in the index also must meet certain liquidity standards that help keep the fund’s costs down.

Emerging Markets Outlook

Many emerging markets around the world have benefited from several robust economic tailwinds over the last 12 to 18 months. These include lower interest rates, declining or moderating inflation, and increased capital investments. Tariffs and trade conflicts with the US have caused some instability, but emerging markets have proven to be remarkably resilient in the face of these challenges. International stocks also benefitted from a weaker US dollar in 2025, something that has not been as large of a factor in 2026.

Much of this year’s gains for IEMG can be connected to increased profitability expectations for tech stocks in Taiwan and South Korea that should benefit from AI spending. The iShares MSCI Taiwan ETF (EWT) has already gained over 27%, year-to-date. Its largest holding is also IEMG’s largest holding, Taiwan Semiconductor. The iShares MSCI South Korea ETF (EWY) is up 49.1% since January 1. EWY's two largest holdings are the second and third largest in IEMG, Samsung Electronics and SK Hynix.

A report that was published just last week by the International Monetary Fund stated that portfolio investments in emerging markets have increased about 700% since the global financial crisis, with most of these investments coming from “nonbanks” like hedge funds, pension funds, and insurance companies. These investments have allowed some emerging markets to show strong returns, but it also dramatically increases their risk. The IMF made the follow statement in its analysis of emerging markets investments:

Portfolio flows to emerging markets tend to be more volatile than bank flows and are increasingly sensitive to global risk conditions, as our analysis shows. Abrupt retrenchments can intensify external financing pressures, raise borrowing costs, and trigger sharp currency depreciations, leading to financial strains that weigh on economic growth. These risks have come to the fore in the context of the war in the Middle East, as several emerging markets are experiencing a reversal of capital flows from nonresident nonbank investors.

Emerging market investments have always come with added risks, but these more recent trends point to the possibility of even greater volatility ahead.

Additional Risk to Consider

Earlier in this analysis I mentioned the heavy concentration in the top five holdings in the fund. Country allocation presents additional risks as over 78% of IEMG is invested in Asia with Taiwan and China comprising over 45% of the fund’s assets on their own. Investors who are confident in the opportunities presented in this part of the world may be better served by owning the iShares MSCI Emerging Markets Asia ETF (EEMA), which invests exclusively in that continent. EEMA has outperformed IEMG over the last 1-, 3-, and 10-year periods. A complete breakdown of the fund’s geographic diversification, or lack thereof, for IEMG is shown below.

Conclusion

There can certainly be a place for a small allocation to emerging markets in a larger, diversified portfolio. Those who do choose to own IEMG should understand the risks involved with their decision. It is interesting to note that even with its recent gains, Morningstar only gives IEMG a three-star rating for risk adjusted returns. SCHF and VEA both receive four stars. Since its inception in October of 2012, the iShares Core MSCI Emerging Markets ETF has only produced an average total return of 5.2%. As I said in my first analysis, meaningful success in owning IEMG depends on market timing, which few seasoned financial experts recommend. My rating of a Hold for the fund comes with the caveat that I still believe international developed markets offer the best risk-return scenario for the remainder of 2026.

data.title: Travelers: Profits Up, Strong Chart, Remains A Lower-Risk Financials Play
data.description:
The Travelers Companies (TRV) reported decent first-quarter revenue and earnings numbers on Thursday, April 16. Shares traded lower initially, but I remain bullish on the now $64 billion market cap Financials sector stock. Along with a notable dividend hike, the major domestic insurance company remains on track to deliver returns to shareholders.

The stock is up 9% since my September 2025 analysis, outperforming the S&P 500 by three percentage points. Today, I reiterate a "Buy" rating. I’ll review the Q1 report, provide a refreshed valuation, and offer an updated view of TRV’s technicals.

TRV Outperforming XLF YoY, Lags SPY

In April, Travelers reported a solid set of quarterly results. Q1 non-GAAP EPS of $7.71 topped the Wall Street consensus forecast by $0.63, while revenue of $10.3 billion, down 1.7% from the same period a year ago, was a $770 million miss. Its consolidated combined ratio came in at 88.6%. Strong underwriting and investment performance came alongside a dividend hike.

But the stock dipped 1%-2% in the premarket on Thursday, April 16, after reporting results. I see that as a buying opportunity. The "sell the news" event came after the options market priced in a 3.5% earnings-related stock price swing based on the at-the-money straddle expiring soonest after the report. Today, implied volatility is modest at 25%, and short interest on the stock is low at 2.1%.

Looking back on the quarter that was, Travelers got off to a strong start in 2026, with net income of $1.71 billion and core income of $1.70 billion. The company tagged a strong return on equity of 21.1% and a core return on equity of 19.7%. Specifically, its underwriting performance was a significant driver, with pre-tax underwriting income of $1.521 billion, marking the sixth consecutive quarter above $1.5 billion and the aforementioned consolidated combined ratio of 88.6%.

The robust operational prints were boosted by lower catastrophe losses of $761 million pre-tax compared with Q1 2025, as well as $413 million in net favorable prior-year reserve development across all segments.

Strategically, the company continued to put its stockholders first in Q1, returning $2.223 billion to shareholders through $1.985 billion in buybacks and $238 million in dividends. Income investors are surely pleased after TRV’s Board of Directors declared a 14% increase in the quarterly dividend to $1.25 per share, marking 22 consecutive years of dividend increases.

Color On Q1

On the balance sheet, the firm remained in a very strong financial position with a debt-to-capital ratio of 21.2%, easily within its target range.

Finally, the investment portfolio is of high quality, with net investment income increasing 9% to $833 million after tax, driven by higher yields in the fixed income portfolio.

Balance Sheet Strength

On the earnings outlook, EPS growth is expected to be about flat this year. That’s nothing to write home about, but out-year per-share earnings may rise 3.3%, with muted gains thereafter. That’s not all that impressive, but the sell-side remains upbeat on TRV, as evidenced by a high 21 EPS upgrades in the past 90 days, compared to just three downgrades.

Of course, profitability trends remain very stout.

Travelers: Revenue & Earnings Forecasts, EPS Revision Trends

On valuation, I have just modest updates to my September 2025 analysis. If we assume $28 of normalized non-GAAP EPS and apply the same 13x price-to-earnings ratio that I went with last time, TRV should trade near $364. That’s about 20% below the current price, keeping TRV in value territory.

It’s possible that an 11x-12x P/E could be fair, based on where the insurance industry lands on its cyclical path. So, I don’t expect to raise my multiple figure any time soon.

TRV: Low P/E Ratio vs. History, Modest P/S

Key risks for Travelers include regulatory worries in the insurance industry, particularly with a rather heavy-handed approach by the Trump administration. Of course, competition remains intense in the industry, especially in personal insurance underwriting, and that could crimp margins and its combined ratio going forward.

Premium rate increases might also be weaker in the years ahead if consumers’ real income growth remains near flat.

Competitor Analysis

Looking ahead, corporate event data from Wall Street Horizon show a projected Q2 2026 earnings date of Thursday, July 16, BMO. Shares trade ex a $1.25 quarterly dividend on Wednesday, June 10.

Corporate Event Risk Calendar

The Technical Take

With an attractive valuation, an EPS beat, and hiked dividends and buybacks, TRV’s technical situation is among the best you’ll come across in the Financials sector. Notice in the chart below that shares are in a protracted uptrend. I see support down at an uptrend support line near the long-term 200-day moving average at $280. I’d also call out that the 200dma is on the rise, suggesting that the bulls control the primary trend. Resistance is at the uptrend resistance line, near $320.

Also take a look at the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph. It has ranged from 30 to 70. This is key, as we’ve seen TRV’s stock price gyrate up and down, but still trend upward. So, buying when the RSI drifts toward 30 and selling when it nears 70 is a trading strategy. Of course, the broader uptrend augurs for a longer-term view (but still a bullish stance). Finally, with a high amount of volume by price below today’s level, there should be ample support if we see a more sustained pullback.

TRV: Bullish Uptrend, Support Near $280, Rising 200dma

The Bottom Line

I have a "Buy" rating on TRV. Shares are compelling on valuation, despite a lackluster growth outlook. After a double-beat Q1 report with favorable, shareholder-accretive activities, the technical situation is very strong.

data.title: LexinFintech Holdings Ltd.: October Regulation Settled, But Structural Decline Remains (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Update

LexinFintech (LX) released its 4Q25 results in March. The results confirmed the impact of the October regulation that was proposed last year. The policy was a serious one as it not only intended to improve macro-prudential stability via stricter consumer lending standards for banks, but also a crack down on predatory lending. This fits with the macro direction of the country amid the doubling down of stimulus measures ever since late-2024. Given uncertainties, I've previously downgraded the stock to "sell" back in January.

In 4Q24, net income was down by 41% YoY, hitting CNY214 million. Meanwhile, revenue was down by 17%, reaching 3 billion. These figures make the situation seem more dire than it actually was. Provisions for contingent liabilities spiked significantly, reaching CNY935 million vs. only 684 million in guarantee income. This is a point of worry given the firm's persistent challenge between the risk it insures against vs. the costs it receives.

Another significant impact from the new policies is towards their tech-empowerment service, which is essentially an asset-light model where the firm helps financial institutions to upgrade the underwriting process without commitments like putting up capital or assuring against credit loses. Revenue from the segment hit CNY170 million vs. 601 million in 4Q24. Its impact on profit were particularly strong given higher margins relative to other business lines.

Latest earnings

The situation: the good and the bad

LX currently has the lowest P/B at 0.22x with a dividend yield of 17%. This makes it the cheapest amongst peers which makes sense for qualitative reasons. The business has the weakest take rate among peers due to its choice of targeting prime customers with decent credit profiles. As a result, competition is fierce against rivalry from banks directly and from larger fintech companies with a size advantage. My previous theses points out the persistent loss between the guarantee income it earns vs. the expenses from assurance payouts. 4Q25 results continues to show this reality and its continuing deterioration.

Deteriorating credit risk trade-off

That said, there were also positive evidence from the recent earnings. Firstly, take rates continue to be robust while the number of funding partners continue to increase. This provides some assurance against the October regulations. The regulation itself will likely not cause significant declines moving forward given that APRs that the firm originally charge were likely lower than the 24% cap.

In one of the segments, the company extends loans through e-commerce channels by embedding its Fenqile platform. Sales from this channel continues to be strong in 4Q25, reaching CNY388 million vs. 345 million a year ago. Overall, these contrast with the extent of regulatory impact on higher-yield and asset-lite lending platform as financial institutions were required to shift these activities in-house. In addition, Lexinfintech is also required to put up 30% of capital. Most of the shock on this end has been materialized, which makes it likely that earnings will be much more resilient after 2025.

Asset quality: A historical view

While delinquency rates have been normalizing since the beginning of 2024, asset quality cannot say the same and has been deteriorating for at least 3 years. Over 86% of loan contribution were from repeat borrowers for FY25. Early on, it was focusing on university graduates that have just entered the job market. The thesis was to capitalized on the opportunity driven by their futures earnings potential while solving their problem of limited access to consumer credit due a lack of track record. These profiles typically have an age range of 18-36. In the 20F of 2022, its target has apparently changed to 23-40 years old.

The increase in average age reflects a reality that the firm is no longer able to attract the same group of graduates or higher-ed students with commensurate debt servicing ability as before. There may be two main causes. Firstly, the landscape has simply became more competitive as traditional banks and incumbents fintech players have been more capable. Second, the original target could be experiencing the structural challenges of youth unemployment, a big deal in the country.

To grow the loan balance, LX expanded its reach to higher quality prime customers slowly. The lack of "edge" continues to be reflected in the persistent gap between its assurance income and its credit loss expense with still lots of uncertainties on how successful the company will navigate the misfit.

Vintage charge-off rates

Valuation

As mentioned earlier, LX has the cheapest valuation against peers for qualitative reasons that reflects the structural challenges mentioned. Contrasting to peers, it suffers from exceeding the 10x leverage cap, unable to underwrite more loans for banks, and also unable to perform aggressive buybacks like its peers. Its cash return to shareholder is pretty much anchored to the 30% payout ratio.

That said, there is no doubt that the stock is significantly undervalued. It trades at levels before the September 2024 stimulus, a key turning point for Chinese equities. The problem is that there is no catalyst at the moment to turnaround its structurally declining business in China. This differs from its peers which either has a much more stable market niche or is expanding aggressively internationally.

Comparisons

Final take

Overall, I am upgrading LX to "hold" vs. the "sell" rating in January. The difference being new evidences from the FY25 results. The magnitude of policy impact is as expected. However, the risks were specific and contained. For LX, its tech-empowerment line was hit the hardest due to stricter requirements on leverage ceiling co-lending capital commitment. Otherwise, its asset-lite business remains stable against the backdrop of structural challenges domestically. I don't think earnings beyond FY25 will shrink drastically but gradually. There is not much that can change at the moment. Valuations are indeed extremely cheap. However, that doesn't necessarily provide a definite "buy" as the company needs to continuously re-iterate its lending approach.

data.title: Marvell: Slower To AI Race, Hasty Rally - Downgrade To Hold
data.description:
I previously rated Marvell Technology, Inc. (NASDAQ:MRVL) as a Buy in November 2025, attributed to its beneficiary custom AI accelerator status through the strategic partnerships with two leading hyperscalers.

In this article, I shall discuss why MRVL has been downgraded as a Hold, with the overly done rally negating its structurally robust, diversified, profitable growth prospects during the multi-year cloud super cycle.

MRVL Proves Its AI Beneficiary Status

Since my last Buy rating upon a dip to the $80s for an improved margin of safety, my Buy Zones have already materialized numerous times in mid November 2025, February 2026, and March 2026, before MRVL charts an outsized stock price rally of +46.4% to record a new 52 week high of $138s.

1. Data Center Monetization Trends

MRVL's recent price action is likely attributed to its two hyperscaler customers signaling the durability of their capex spending trends entering 2026, such as:

  • Amazon (AMZN) at the CapEx guidance of $200 billion in FY2026 (+55.8% YoY) and
  • Microsoft (MSFT) announcing numerous data center-related projects over the past few months after reporting YTD capex of $49.27B (+60.3% YoY).

These developments are likely to be accretive to MRVL's top/bottom-line growth prospects, with it building upon their historical data center revenue growth from $2.4B in FY2023 to $6.1B in FY2026, expanding at a 3Y CAGR of +36.4%.

The same has already been embedded in their FY2027 data center revenue growth guidance by +40% YoY and FY2028 by +50% YoY, with it building upon FY2026 levels of +46.7% YoY.

With data center opportunities comprising an expanded ratio of MRVL's overall revenues at 74.3% in FY2026 (+2.3 points YoY/+40.1 from FY2021 levels of 34.2%) and two of its hyperscaler clients hinting at further investments, I can understand why the stock has outperformed as it has thus far.

This is especially since the management has highlighted the deepened, strategic partnership and cross-selling trends with its two hyperscaler customers across the existing/next-gen XPU platforms, with it lending credibility to AMZN-MRVL's previously expanded 5Y multi-generational agreement.

Combined with the new strategic partnership with Nvidia (NVDA), allowing MRVL to supply "custom AI accelerators, or XPUs, and NVLink Fusion-compatible scale-up networking" to complement the former's rack-scale AI compute offerings, it is apparent that MRVL's custom AI opportunities may further expand from current levels.

2. Interconnection/Switching Opportunities

Otherwise, readers may want to note that the custom AI chip business remains a relatively small portion of MRVL's overall revenues at approximately $1.5B in FY2026 (doubled YoY), or the equivalent ~18.3% (+5.3 points YoY).

The same has been observed in the management's guidance of the "custom revenue to grow more than 20% year-over-year in fiscal 2027."

These reasons are also why MRVL's growth prospects lie in their other data center offerings, including the:

  • optical digital signal processors [DSP],
  • active electrical cable [AEC],
  • PCIe retimers,
  • data center interconnect [DCI] optical modules, and
  • Ethernet switching silicon solutions,

with these comprising the bulk of its revenues at an estimated sum of $4.59B in FY2026 (+34% YoY).

If anything, MRVL has guided a similar development for FY2027 in the recent earnings call, with:

  • the "combined AEC and retimer revenue to more than double year-over-year in fiscal 2027," and
  • the data center switching to grow from $300M in FY2026 to over $600M in FY2027, up from the last guidance of $500M,

with their "interconnect business to significantly outpace cloud CapEx growth" at over +50% YoY, up from the last guidance of +30% YoY.

These numbers underscore their robust and diversified growth prospects, no matter the lumpiness of the custom AI programs, with it well mitigating the prior rumors of Trainium3/Trainium4 contracts supposedly lost to Alchip Technologies.

These reasons may also be why MRVL has further invested in their interconnection and switching capabilities through the recent acquisitions, including:

  • Celestial AI, to expand their "optical interconnect technology for scale-up connectivity" in the "next-generation AI and cloud data centers," and
  • XConn Technologies, to expand their "advanced PCIe and CXL switching silicon" portfolio,

allowing them to drive further growth prospects in the two end markets moving forward, as similarly observed in the projected expansion in the DCI pluggable optics Total Addressable Market [TAM] "by more than 5x by calendar 2030."

3. Forward Guidance

These reasons may also be why MRVL has felt confident to offer a promising FY2028 revenue guidance of approximately $15B (expanding at a 2Y CAGR of +35.3%) and FY2028 adj EPS guidance of "well over $5" (expanding at a 2Y CAGR of +32.6%).

Combined with the diversified growth prospects across the custom AI chip and interconnection/switching segments, it is unsurprising that the consensus have already raised their forward estimates.

This is with MRVL expected to report an accelerated top/bottom-line growth estimates at a CAGR of +32.5%/+38.2% through FY2029, against the historical growth profile at a 5Y CAGR of +22.5%/+25.3 and a 10Y CAGR of +11.6%/+22.6%, respectively.

While these numbers may appear underwhelming compared to its direct accelerator peers reporting triple digits growth prospects, ie:

  • NVDA at "$1 trillion plus of Blackwell plus Rubin" revenues at the end of 2027 and
  • Broadcom (AVGO) at an excellent "AI revenue from chips, just chips, in excess of $100 billion in 2027,"

I am of the opinion that MRVL remains well positioned to deliver robust (albeit slower), profitable growth trends moving forward.

4. Expensive Valuations

Thanks to the +179.8% stock price rally from the 52 weeks low and the +53.2% rally from the March 2026 bottom, it is unsurprising that MRVL is rather inflated at FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 35.01x.

This is up from the 1Y mean of 25.09x and the sector median of 22.84x, albeit still near to the 5Y mean of 36.48x.

Thanks to management's promising guidance through FY2028 and the consequently robust adj EPS growth prospects at a 3Y CAGR of +38.2%, I am of the opinion that MRVL remains compellingly valued at my estimated 3Y PEG non-GAAP ratio of 0.91x, against the sector median of 1.38x and the 5Y mean of 1.52x.

Otherwise, when comparing MRVL's FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 35.01x and my estimated 3Y PEG non-GAAP ratio of 0.91x against its direct AI accelerator peers, including:

  • general - NVDA at 23.66x/0.56x,
  • general - Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) at 38.13x/0.72x, and
  • custom - AVGO at 33.41x/0.68x, respectively,

it appears that MRVL's recent rally may have occurred rather fast and furious, given the slight premium against some of its peers.

Part of the premium may be attributed to MRVL's notably lower net debt position of $1.83B in FY2026 (+41.1% YoY) and a net debt to adj EBITDA ratio of 0.56x (against a year ago at 1.58x), thanks to the richer profit margins and robust free cash flow generation.

While these numbers may pale in comparison to its cash rich peers, such as:

  • NVDA at a net debt to adj EBITDA ratio of -0.38x in FY2026 (given the higher cash than debt),
  • AMD at -0.86x in FY2025, and
  • AVGO at 0.98x in FQ1'26,

I believe that MRVL's healthier balance sheet is worth celebrating, since further outperformance is likely as they drive double-digits data center-related growth prospects moving forward.

MRVL Is Downgraded To Hold Here

Thanks to the near vertical recovery over the past two weeks, MRVL has already hit a new 52 week high of $138s, with the overly fast and furious rally seemingly overly done, as similarly observed in the somewhat premium valuations.

My caution is attributed to the stock trading well beyond my fair value estimates of $112 as well, based on the annualized FQ4'26 adj EPS performance at $3.20 (+33.3% YoY) and the FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 35.01x.

On the one hand, there appears to be an excellent near doubling upside potential to my long-term, MRVL price target of $262.50, based on the consensus FY2029 adj EPS estimates of $7.50.

This development is not overly aggressive as well, given the management's promising double digits data center growth guidance through FY2028.

On the other hand, MRVL has entered overbought territories by the time of writing, as observed in the inflated RSIs and trading volumes, with it potentially triggering a profit taking cadence.

Despite their structurally robust, profitable growth prospects during the multi-year cloud super cycle, I believe that investors may be better off waiting for a moderate correction nearer to the prior support levels of $110s for an improved margin of safety, with those levels also nearer to my fair value estimates of $112.

If anything, readers may want to pay attention to MRVL's uptrend support line since early 2023, with historical trends implying a potential intermediate term correction to the 50/100/200 day moving averages of $80s/$90s.

As a result of the relatively steep downside risk of -27.5% at the midpoint from the overly done rally and the somewhat expensive valuations, I believe that a downgrade to a Hold rating may be quite prudent here.

Do not chase this rally over the cliff.

data.title: Bank of New York Mellon: Margin Expansion Drives Results Again In Q1
data.description:
Shares of Bank of New York Mellon (BK) have been an extraordinary performer over the past year, gaining 70%. BK’s low-risk business model has performed very well, saddling it with little of the private credit exposure that has weighed on some financials. At the same time, it has very effectively managed costs, and it is also benefiting from fewer expected Fed rate cuts. I have been too cautious in my coverage of BNY, as valuation has kept me at a “Hold” while strong results have powered continued outperformance, meriting a “Buy.” I last covered shares in January, and the stock is up 7% since then. With updated financials, now is a good time to revisit BK.

Cost discipline has been excellent

In the company’s first quarter, BNY Mellon earned $2.24, which beat expectations by $0.31 as revenue surged 13%. The company has done a fantastic job in controlling costs and creating significant operating leverage. As a result, margins increased by 600bps to 37%. Compared to the other custody banks, State Street (STT) and Northern Trust (NTRS), I view BNY Mellon as the clear leader in terms of operational execution and cost management.

As you can see below, the company is generating about 20% more revenue per employee than just four years ago, and most of this has flowed to the bottom line, with pre-tax income per employee up 44%. These benefits are largely before any benefits from AI, and the process-oriented nature of custody banking creates ample automation opportunity. It highlighted that 40% of code is now written by AI, enabling a 10% increase in software releases during Q1. 25% of new onboarding processes are enabled by AI, and BK has cut processing times by 20%. While I still believe most of the margin expansion has already occurred, these innovations can help propel some further momentum.

A key metric I have tracked to measure operating efficiency is its profitability excluding net interest income. In Q1, pre-tax profit ex interest was $646 million. Last year, it was $363 million. That is a 78% increase, thanks to the fact that fee revenue grew 11% while expenses were up just 5%. It used to be that the company struggled to make a profit on just fee revenue and was solely reliant on interest income for its profitability. Cost cuts have diversified its income stream, creating a much more resilient business in my view.

Rising markets lift custody results

Drilling into segment results, its Securities Services business, which is its traditional custody operation, grew fees 10% to $1.4 billion and revenue by 17%. Pre-tax profits surged 46% to $1 billion. As a reminder, as a custody bank, BK does not do traditional lending and instead holds assets on behalf of clients, making it key to the “plumbing” of the financial system. This is a very low-fee risk, as it is more operational than financial in nature. Assets under custody in this division were up 12.7% to $42.7 trillion.

As has been consistently the case, assets are rising more quickly than fees. This speaks to the highly competitive nature of the custody business and the fact that fee rates tend to fall as client account values grow (as the cost of holding $1.2 billion of assets is little different from $1.0 billion). Rising markets are the primary driver of its asset growth and are a tailwind to revenue. Additionally, growing corporate debt issuance boosted its issuer services revenue by 4%. One standout was a 44% surge in FX trading revenue, given volatility in March. I expect growth to slow in Q2, given a difficult comparison.

In Market and Wealth Services, BK grew revenue by 11% to $1.9 billion, and profits grew 18% to $961 million thanks to 300bps of margin expansion. Assets under custody/administration are now $16.5 billion, up about 11% from last year. Clearance and collateral management were up 19%, as collateral balances and trading volumes have been very strong. Securities lending has been a key growth driver for the sector as clients seek to enhance yields, creating a new revenue opportunity for BK. FX revenue was also up 24% here to $36 million, thanks to strong trading levels.

Spending discipline supports investment management results

Its investment and wealth management business has been the relative laggard, and revenue was up just 6% from last year to $825 million. AUM was also up 6% to $2.1 trillion, and this was entirely due to the market. Net flows were -$17 billion, though its active strategies were flat. Active management has faced significant pressure from passive strategies, and BK has consistently struggled to generate inflows in this environment. I expect that to remain the case and for it to be dependent on a rising market to boost AUM. That said, its cost control efforts are now showing more clearly in this segment, with expenses up just 2%. As a result, profits jumped 43% to $90 million. Margins remain relatively weak at 11%, just a fraction of its custody operation.

Finally, net interest income was up 18% to $1.37 billion. Deposits were up 13% to $318 billion. These deposits are mostly cash swept in its custody business, and BK does not have traditional retail deposits. I am particularly encouraged to see non-interest-bearing balances up 14% from last year, and we have more definitively passed the bottom. A favorable deposit mix plus the ability to reinvest maturities at higher yields helped to expand net interest margin ("NIM") 8bps from last year. NIM was flat sequentially, and I expect it to continue running in this area over the balance of the year.

BK has a very low-risk balance sheet

During the quarter, it released $7 million of loan loss reserves, and I view BK as one of the most resilient companies to any challenges in credit quality. Of its $462 million in assets, it has just $81 billion in loans, primarily as a participant in syndicated loans to large, investment-grade corporations. It holds $153 billion in cash, and it has $154 billion of securities. 96% of this portfolio is AA or AAA rated, and 4% is A- to A+ rated. That 4% bucket is entirely government debt of non-US countries where it operates. This is an incredibly low-risk portfolio, and it is simply not exposed to credit losses like other banks, making it a relative safe haven.

The company ended the quarter with an 11% tier 1 common equity (CET1) capital ratio, which was down 90bps sequentially. While still a strong level, this was a surprisingly large drop, and it is mostly attributed to a surge in overnight lending on March 31, and quarter-end short-term lending can be distortive. This would have reversed on April 1 and increased capital. Speaking to the temporary nature of the decline, BK announced a new $10 billion buyback program. It did $983 million of buybacks in Q1, and this new program will likely last two years. Thanks to buybacks, its share count is down about 4% over the past year. Shares also offer a 1.6% dividend yield.

Its valuation is justified, and shares remain a "Hold"

Back in January, I was targeting about $8.25 this year. I now expect at least $8.60 in earnings, assuming markets remain around current levels. That leaves shares over 15x earnings, which is a relatively hefty multiple. BK continues to get a D- from Seeking Alpha’s valuation screener.

While this valuation is a clear reason to be cautious, I do not believe it merits a "Sell" rating. BK has far less exposure to credit losses than typical banks, and its income is much more fee-based. While impacted by market fluctuations, the nature of its revenue makes results much steadier than other banks. It also has done a remarkable job of enhancing margins, reducing its reliance on interest rates to make money. These factors merit a premium valuation. I struggle to see much potential for multiple expansion from here, which is why I am retaining a “Hold” rating rather than a “Buy,” as ~15x is fair in my view. With ongoing buybacks and little credit exposure, I see no rush to sell, even after such a large run.

data.title: GRNY: Making Appropriate Moves Thus Far In 2026
data.description:
Late 2025 was the last time I reviewed the Fundstrat Granny Shots US Large Cap ETF (GRNY). At that time I thought Tom Lee and his team at Fundstrat had set the fund up to succeed in 2026 especially given the allocation to many AI related stocks.

Let’s dive into the ETF’s performance thus far in 2026 and determine if the fund has readjusted accordingly given the significant macro events that have already transpired in just the first quarter of 2026.

Fund Strategy

As I’ve mentioned in prior articles, this ETF is focused around a few short-term and long-term themes. The short-term themes include: style tilt, seasonality, and PMI recovery. The long-term themes include Energy/Cyber security, millennials, global labor suppliers and easing financial conditions. Of these themes, I am particularly bullish on the long-term themes associated with energy/cyber security and Millennials as I do believe in the “Great Wealth Transfer” in which Millennials will begin to inherit assets from the Baby Boomer generation. Also, as AI will continue to make waves in the years and decades to come, the need for energy will be even greater as will be the need for companies and individuals to protect their sensitive information.

Not only do I like many of these themes, but I like that to be included in the fund, the stock must fit at least two of these themes (if not more). To me this is a much more detailed and strategic approach to investing compared to say the ARK Innovation ETF (ARKK), where any company can be considered exciting and innovative. I mean who knew John Deere (DE) was rewriting their playbook?

Let’s now discuss the fund’s current holdings and how GRNY has performed thus far in 2026.

Performance & Fund Structure

Let’s begin by comparing GRNY to a few similar ETFs, like ARKK and the Dan IVES Wedbush AI Revolution ETF (IVES) and some broader ETFs like the Invesco QQQ Trust, Series 1 ETF (QQQ) and the Vanguard 500 Index Fund ETF (VOO).

As you can see GRNY has managed to outperform all of those other ETFs thanks in part to some adjustments in the fund's top holdings.

As of March 31, the fund’s top ten holdings were as follows:

Since my last article, there have been some significant changes with GRNY’s top ten holdings. In my prior article, GRNY was heavy into tech with Broadcom (AVGO) and much of the Mag 7 holding those top ten slots. Now, you can see GRNY has pivoted hard into energy with Chevron (CVX) and Texas Pacific Land (TPL) now holding more weight within GRNY.

I think it’s fair to argue, Lee and his team are making these changes due to macro conditions, like the ongoing conflict in Iran and rising oil prices, and are making these stocks fit into the aforementioned themes.

However with GRNY and ARKK you are paying a premium (which I’ll discuss more below) for these ETFs and so if you’re paying a premium then I’d argue you want a fund manager who’s on top of the macro picture rather than a fund that’s sticking to their guns and is unwilling to make appropriate adjustments. To go into GRNY’s holdings further you can see this ETF is much more diverse now with roughly 8% of the fund allocated to energy:

In my prior article, the top three investing sectors for GRNY were also Technology, Industrials, and Financials, with 72% of capital being allocated to those three sectors. Now, that allocation has dropped down to 57% illustrating the necessary changes Lee and his team made during the quarter.

Furthermore, taking a look at all of the fund’s holdings, outside of Palantir (PLTR) GRNY is completely out on software SaaS companies. This was one issue I had during my analysis of another ETF I reviewed, VanEck Morningstar Wide Moat ETF (MOAT), was the inclusion of some very questionable stocks like Salesforce (CRM) who could easily be seriously disrupted by AI. Now, I do believe there’s some excessive fear in the market stoked by Anthropic’s Claude enhancements like Claude Cowork and I do believe some SaaS companies like Crowdstrike (CRWD), Datadog (DDOG) and others can survive and perhaps even thrive with advanced AI capabilities.

Regardless of how well SaaS software companies perform I do like that GRNY is more diverse now as the ETF currently has exposure to ten different sectors.

Comparing VOO to GRNY, VOO also has a significant allocation going towards technology and VOO has capital going to all sectors:

ARKK on the other hand currently has more capital going into Health Care compared to VOO and GRNY and also only has exposure to six sectors:

Part of the reason I believe GRNY has outperformed VOO and ARKK YTD is because of their selective industrials exposure. Stocks like GE Vernova (GEV) and Quanta Services (PWR) have done very well thus far in 2026. To me this further proves that Lee and his team have not only determined an appropriate thematic strategy for this ETF but they are nailing the stock selections for their themes.

Fees and Risks

Moving on to fees, I did mention above that GRNY has a higher expense ratio than the median for all ETFs as you can see below:

Like GRNY, ARKK also carries an expense ratio of 0.75%. Conversely, VOO has an expense ratio of 0.03% which makes it the most attractive of three ETFs from a cost perspective.

As a long-term investor when it comes to ETFs I’m focused on two key aspects, minimizing fees and reducing my risk. So let’s next turn to the risk associated with these ETFs.

GRNY has moderate risk I would say as you can see the fund has a higher turnover percentage compared to the median for all ETFs:

Usually I don’t like to see the turnover percentage this high but performance to date is showing they have been making the right adjustments. I also like that GRNY only has ~28% of capital allocated to their top ten holdings which is much lower than the median for all ETFs.

ARKK on the other hand is far riskier as you can see below:

Cathie Wood and her team have always held a high turnover percentage which is part of why I’ve steered clear of the fund. Additionally, compared to GRNY and other ETFs, ARKK has a major amount of capital going to their top ten holdings. I’ve stated this before but their Tesla position is far too large for my liking.

VOO is certainly the least risky of the three ETFs with a very low turnover percentage and the percentage of capital going to the ETF’s top ten holdings is also below the median for all ETFs:

Conclusion

I maintain that Tom Lee and his team at Fundstrat have an excellent strategy and are executing on that strategy with their thematic stock selection.

Despite the higher turnover percentage compared to prior periods I’d say these moves are working out given the ETF’s performance. I can also appreciate that, if you are willing to pay higher fees, you better be getting your money’s worth. I believe Lee is certainly making appropriate adjustments given the macro conditions.

I remain bullish on GRNY given the fund’s performance in 2025 and thus far in 2026 and feel confident in the fund’s ongoing investment thesis.

data.title: We (Still) Don't Expect The Bank Of England To Hike Rates
data.description:
By James Smith, Developed Markets Economist, UK

No, the UK is not more vulnerable to the coming inflation shock

Financial markets are still applying the 2022 playbook to the Bank of England. The scale of repricing in interest rate expectations has been more dramatic than either the eurozone or the US. The implied UK rate one year from now has risen a full percentage point since the onset of war, and investors are still pricing close to two hikes by year-end.

That seems to imply that the UK is facing a more severe inflation shock than elsewhere. We disagree. That may have been true in 2022. And Britain may be a major energy importer. But its dependency is on natural gas. And fortunately, this is not yet a natural gas crisis. Wholesale prices are still a fraction of where they traded in the fallout of the Ukraine invasion.

Based on ING’s updated energy price forecasts, we think inflation is set to rise to 3% through May/June and bounce between 3.5-4% from July onwards. That’s virtually identical to our new eurozone inflation predictions. And if anything, the UK may be less exposed to the second-round inflationary effects that keep central bankers up at night.

The jobs market is weaker than in much of Europe, judging by vacancy data, thanks to the effects of last year’s tax rises. Those increases had minimal impact on inflation but a notable impact on hiring. It’s a reminder that firms have much less pricing power than they did in 2022. Likewise, workers don’t have nearly as much power to demand higher pay. Wage growth has already fallen sharply in recent months. Unemployment is set to rise further.

We're still not convinced the Bank will hike rates

Fiscal policy is also getting tighter. The structural deficit is still set to fall this year thanks to the freeze in income tax brackets. That’s in contrast to the post-pandemic period, but also Germany and its neighbouring economies, which are set to benefit from the wave of infrastructure spending coming online this year. Universal energy bill support looks unlikely.

The other major difference is that Bank of England interest rates are still widely seen as restrictive. The nominal neutral rate – a rate neither expansionary nor restrictive for the economy – is seen around 2.5-3%. Again, that’s a difference with the ECB, where its 2% deposit rate is largely seen as neutral. The centre of gravity is still down, if anything; remember the Bank of England was on the verge of cutting rates further before the war began.

Finally, the Bank of England itself has begun to strike a different tone to its continental neighbours. Governor Bailey recently made a rare and not-so-subtle intervention on market pricing, saying investors were “getting ahead of themselves” in pricing hikes.

We took this to mean an April rate hike is highly unlikely. We’re sticking with our call for the Bank to keep rates on hold this year. This marks a small divergence with the ECB, from which our team now expects a hike in June.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

Original Post
